intro:
Transjordan — in Hebrew be-ʿeber ha-Yarden, "beyond the Jordan" — is the long belt of highland and plateau east of the Jordan River and the Dead Sea, reaching from the Yarmuk in the north down to the gorge of the Arnon. It embraces three sub-regions: the heights of Bashan in the north, the wooded hills of Gilead in the center, and the level Mishor tableland in the south. Well-watered and rich in pasture, it was claimed at Israel's request by the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, whose great herds suited its grasslands (Numbers 32:1).

Israel's conquest of the land actually began here, before the Jordan was ever crossed. Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan were defeated in turn (Numbers 21:23; Numbers 21:33), and their territory was apportioned to the eastern tribes on the solemn condition that their fighting men first help conquer Canaan (Numbers 32:20). From the plains of Moab at Shittim, Israel's last camp (Numbers 25:1), Moses surveyed the Promised Land from Mount Nebo on the ridge of Pisgah and died there, permitted to see but not to enter (Deuteronomy 34:1; Deuteronomy 34:5).

The region is woven through the wider story. Here Jacob wrestled through the night at the ford of the Jabbok and was renamed Israel, calling the place Peniel — "I have seen God face to face" (Genesis 32:30); here angels met him at Mahanaim (Genesis 32:2). Later Jephthah the Gileadite judged Israel (Judges 11:1), Elijah the Tishbite came from its settlers (1 Kings 17:1), and David found refuge at Mahanaim during Absalom's revolt, sustained by Barzillai the Gileadite (2 Samuel 17:27). Exposed on Israel's frontier, the eastern tribes were also the first to be carried into exile, swept away by Tiglath-pileser of Assyria (1 Chronicles 5:26).

Above all, Transjordan is the land of the threshold — the place of waiting, of seeing from afar, and of crossing over into rest. It was fitting, then, that John baptized "in Bethany beyond the Jordan" (John 1:28), and that Jesus withdrew there, where many came to him and believed (John 10:40; John 10:42). As Joshua once led Israel out of this wilderness threshold through the Jordan into the inheritance, so Christ, the true Joshua, leads his people through the waters of death into the rest that Moses longed for and only the Son can give.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Heshbon
Capital of Sihon, taken as Israel advanced (Num 21:26)
Dibon
Town built by Gad on the southern plateau (Num 32:34)
Ramoth-gilead
City of refuge long fought over with Aram (Josh 20:8)
Mahanaim
Where angels met Jacob; later David's wartime refuge (Gen 32:2)
Jabbok
River where Jacob wrestled until dawn (Gen 32:22)
Mount Nebo
Height from which Moses viewed the land and died (Deut 34:1)
Pisgah
Ridge of Nebo overlooking the Promised Land (Deut 3:27)
Shittim
Last camp in the plains of Moab before the crossing (Num 25:1)
Succoth
Where Jacob built booths on his return (Gen 33:17)
Penuel
Where Jacob saw God face to face (Gen 32:30)
Jabesh-gilead
Town Saul rescued from the Ammonites (1 Sam 11:1)
Aroer
Town on the Arnon marking the southern bound (Deut 2:36)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places appear on the map above.

cells: Jacob
Wrestled at the Jabbok and was renamed Israel (Gen 32:28)
Moses
Viewed the land from Nebo and died in Moab (Deut 34:5)
Sihon
Amorite king of Heshbon defeated by Israel (Num 21:23)
Og
Giant king of Bashan, also defeated (Num 21:33)
Balaam
Hired in the plains of Moab to curse Israel (Num 22:5)
Reuben
Tribe granted pastureland east of the Jordan (Num 32:33)
Gad
Tribe settled in Gilead with its great herds (Num 32:34)
Jephthah
Gileadite judge who delivered Israel (Judg 11:1)
Elijah
The Tishbite, from the settlers of Gilead (1 Kgs 17:1)
Aram (Syria)
Fought Israel for Ramoth-gilead and the plateau (1 Kgs 22:3)
Assyria
Tiglath-pileser exiled the eastern tribes first (1 Chr 5:26)
